pmos

How Databases Work — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A product manager's (PM's) working mental model of the database — the system that stores your product's data and decides how fast, how safely, and how cheaply you can read and write it. It walks from the basic vocabulary (tables, rows, queries) through the choices that actually land on a roadmap: relational versus NoSQL, what a schema commits you to, why an index makes one query fast and a write slightly slower, what a transaction guarantees, and the unavoidable trade-off between consistency and availability when your data lives on more than one machine. Written for any PM. Every term is defined the first time it appears, and every acronym is spelled out on first use. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the Wikipedia database corpus and MongoDB's reference docs. 22 verified sources.

What this is (one paragraph)

A database is an organized collection of your product's data, plus the software that manages it. Wikipedia draws the line cleanly: "a database is an organized collection of data... based on the use of a database management system (DBMS), the software that interacts with end users, applications, and the database itself to capture and analyze the data" (Database). In everyday speech people say "the database" to mean both the data and the engine; this primer keeps them separate where the distinction matters. The DBMS is a piece of infrastructure your engineers chose — Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, DynamoDB, and so on — and that choice quietly shapes what your product can promise users about speed, accuracy, and scale. You do not need to write queries to make good calls here. You need to understand the handful of trade-offs that turn into roadmap decisions, incidents, and cost lines.

Why a PM should care

Database choices look like pure engineering, but they decide things product owns. Whether two users can ever see different account balances, whether search stays fast as the table grows from ten thousand rows to ten million, whether a new feature ships in a week or a month, and how much the monthly cloud bill climbs — all of these trace back to database structure. When an engineer says "that requires a schema migration" or "we can't guarantee that's consistent across regions," they are describing constraints from this primer. A PM who can follow the conversation asks better questions, scopes features realistically, and does not promise a customer something the data model cannot deliver. You are not being asked to architect the database. You are being asked to recognize when a product decision is really a database decision in disguise — and most of the expensive ones are.

Core concepts (with definitions)

A small vocabulary covers most database conversations. Define these once and the rest of the primer reads easily.

  • Table — a grid of related records. Formally, "a table is a set of data elements (values) using a model of vertical columns and horizontal rows" (Table (database)). Think of one table per kind of thing: a users table, an orders table.
  • Row (also called a record) — one entry in a table. One user, one order.
  • Column (also called a field) — one attribute every row has, such as email or created_at.
  • Query — a request to read or change data ("give me every order from this user in the last 30 days").
  • Schema — the blueprint that says which tables and columns exist and how they relate (its own section below).
  • DBMS — the database management system, the engine that stores the data and runs the queries.
  • Transaction — a bundle of changes treated as one all-or-nothing unit (its own section below).

One more distinction shapes which database a team reaches for: the workload. Systems tuned for many small live reads and writes — placing an order, updating a profile — are called OLTP (online transaction processing). Systems tuned for heavy analytical questions over huge histories — "revenue by region by quarter" — are called OLAP (online analytical processing), where, as Wikipedia notes, the engine "is generally optimized for read-only operations" (Online transaction processing). The same data often lives in both: an OLTP database runs the product, and a copy flows into an OLAP warehouse for analytics.

The relational model (tables and keys)

Most products start on a relational database — data spread across tables that link to each other. The idea is older than it looks: the relational model was introduced by Edgar Codd in 1969 as "an approach to managing data using a structure and language consistent with first-order predicate logic" (Relational model). In plainer terms, Wikipedia summarizes: "A relational model organizes data into one or more tables (or 'relations') of columns and rows, with a unique key identifying each row" (Relational database). The power is in the links. Instead of repeating a customer's name on every one of their orders, you store the customer once and have each order point back to them. That pointer is a key, and keys are how the relational model keeps data tidy and non-repetitive.

SQL and querying

You talk to a relational database in SQL (Structured Query Language), the long-standing standard for reading and changing relational data. Its original breakthrough was working in sets rather than one record at a time — SQL "introduced the concept of accessing many records with one single command" (SQL). A query like "every order over $100 from the last week, newest first" is a single statement, not a loop an engineer writes by hand. A useful thing for a PM to internalize: SQL is declarative. You describe the result you want, and the database figures out how to get it. That "how" is handled by the query optimizer, an automated planner whose "purpose... is to find the way to process a given query in minimum time" (Query optimization). This is why the same question can be fast or slow depending on indexes and data size, even when the SQL is unchanged — the optimizer's options depend on what you have given it to work with.

Further reading: hellopm.co — Definitive SQL guide for product managers explaining relational databases, data retrieval, filtering, aggregates, joins, and how SQL enables PMs to run analyses independently and make faster data-driven decisions.

Further reading: towardsdatascience.com — Your home for data science and AI.

Further reading: technically.dev — Beginner-friendly guide to SQL covering database schemas, query structure, and core keywords SELECT, FROM, WHERE, and ORDER BY, using a DTC e-commerce example to illustrate concepts.

Schemas and data modeling

The schema is the database's blueprint — the agreed structure of tables, columns, and relationships. Wikipedia's definition is sweeping on purpose: "the schema defines the tables, fields, relationships, views, indexes, packages, procedures, functions, queues, triggers, types, sequences, materialized views, synonyms" and more (Database schema). For product purposes, focus on three pieces.

First, the primary key: the column (or columns) that uniquely identifies each row — "a designated set of attributes (column(s)) that can reliably identify and distinguish between each individual record in a table" (Primary key). Every user gets one stable id. Second, the foreign key: the column that links one table to another by referencing that other table's key — formally "a set of attributes that references a candidate key" (Foreign key). The user_id on an order is a foreign key pointing at the users table. Third, normalization: the practice of splitting data so nothing important is stored twice, designed to "free the collection of relations from undesirable insertion, update and deletion dependencies" (Database normalization). Store a customer's address once, not on every order, and a change updates in one place. The PM-relevant catch: a relational schema is a commitment. Adding or reshaping columns on a large table is a migration, and migrations take engineering time and carry risk — which is exactly why "can we just add a field?" sometimes gets a surprisingly heavy answer.

Indexes — why queries are fast or slow

An index is a secondary data structure the database keeps so it can find rows without scanning the whole table — like the index at the back of a book versus reading every page. The trade-off is explicit: an index "improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a database table at the cost of additional writes and storage space" (Database index). Reads on indexed columns get dramatically faster; every write has to update the index too, so writes get slightly slower and the database grows. This single mechanism explains a huge share of real performance conversations. When a list view "got slow as we grew," the usual fix is an index on the column being filtered or sorted. When someone says "we can't index everything," they mean each index has an ongoing write-and-storage cost. As a PM you do not choose indexes, but knowing they are the lever turns "the page is slow" into a specific, solvable question rather than a vague complaint.

Transactions and ACID

A transaction bundles several changes into one unit that either fully happens or fully does not. The defining property is atomicity: a transaction must be "atomic (it must either be complete in its entirety or have no effect whatsoever)" (Database transaction). The classic example is moving money: debit one account and credit another must both succeed or both fail — never one without the other. The full reliability contract is captured by the acronym ACID, which stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. Under ACID, each transaction "is treated as a single 'unit', which either succeeds completely or fails completely" (ACID). In plain terms: atomicity is all-or-nothing; consistency means the data obeys its rules before and after; isolation means concurrent transactions do not corrupt each other; durability means once it is committed, a crash will not lose it. For a PM, ACID is the difference between "we can guarantee the balance is always right" and "balances might briefly disagree." Any feature touching money, inventory, or bookings leans on these guarantees, so it is worth knowing whether your database provides them by default.

Relational vs NoSQL

The headline architectural fork is relational versus NoSQL ("not only SQL") — a family of non-relational databases that drop fixed tables for more flexible formats. NoSQL "scales easily to manage large, often unstructured datasets," using key-value pairs, documents, and graphs instead of strict rows and columns (NoSQL). The traditional framing was a clean swap: relational gives you strict structure and strong ACID guarantees; NoSQL gives you flexible structure and easier horizontal scaling, historically by relaxing some of those guarantees. That framing is now dated and worth flagging as evolving — the line has blurred. MongoDB's reference docs make the point directly: "While early NoSQL databases lacked ACID compliance, modern systems like MongoDB provide full support for ACID transactions at scale" (MongoDB — ACID transactions). The practical reading for a PM: this is a trade-off space, not a religious war. Relational remains the safe default for structured, relationship-heavy, correctness-critical data; NoSQL earns its place for flexible-shape data, very high scale, or access patterns that map poorly to tables. The right answer is workload-specific, and reasonable engineers disagree.

Types of NoSQL

"NoSQL" is an umbrella, not one thing. The two most common shapes a PM will hear about:

  • Document stores keep each record as a self-contained document — "self-contained units that encapsulate and encode data in a standardized format" such as JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) (Document-oriented database). Because each document carries its own structure, different records can have different fields without a migration — handy for fast-changing or varied data. MongoDB is the best-known example.
  • Key-value stores are the simplest shape: a giant dictionary that fetches a value by its key and nothing fancier. They treat "the value as opaque to the database itself, and typically support only simple operations such as storing, retrieving, updating, and deleting a value by its key" (Key-value database). That narrowness is the point — they are extremely fast for caching and session data. Redis and DynamoDB are common examples.

There are others — wide-column stores and graph databases among them — but document and key-value cover the large majority of product conversations. The selection logic is the same each time: match the store's natural shape and access pattern to the data, rather than picking by reputation.

Scaling: replication and sharding

When one server can no longer hold the load, databases grow in two complementary ways, and the words are worth knowing because they show up in incident reviews and capacity planning.

Replication keeps multiple copies of the same data on different servers, mainly for availability and read capacity. A common arrangement: "In single-leader (also called primary/replica) replication, one database instance is designated as the leader (primary), which handles all write operations" while copies serve reads (Replication (computing)). If the leader fails, a replica can take over. Sharding instead splits the rows themselves across servers, so no single machine holds everything: "Each shard may be held on a separate database server instance in order to spread [load] across multiple servers" (Shard (database architecture)). Replication copies the whole dataset; sharding partitions it. Most large systems use both. The PM-relevant consequence is that the moment your data lives on more than one machine, a new class of trade-off appears — the subject of the next section.

Further reading: medium.com — Engineering deep-dive into how Prefixy built a scalable prefix search service to power autocomplete functionality, covering architecture and design decisions.

Further reading: geeksforgeeks.org — System design walkthrough for a BookMyShow-style ticket booking platform covering functional and non-functional requirements, seat reservation locking, aggregator-theatre integration strategies, and high-concurrency handling.

CAP theorem and consistency trade-offs

Once data is replicated across machines, networks between them can fail, and the CAP theorem describes the corner you are forced into. CAP stands for Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance, and its hard rule is: "if there is a network partition, one has to choose between consistency or availability" (CAP theorem). A network partition is simply two parts of your system temporarily unable to talk. When that happens you either refuse some requests to keep every copy in agreement (favor consistency) or keep serving and let copies briefly diverge (favor availability). You cannot have both during the partition.

The everyday version of "favor availability" is eventual consistency: copies may disagree for a moment but catch up, so that "a system that has achieved eventual consistency is said to have converged, or achieved replica convergence" (Eventual consistency). This is the source of the familiar "I posted it but my friend can't see it yet" lag. For a PM this is a real product decision, not a backend detail: a bank balance demands consistency, while a like-count or social feed can tolerate a few seconds of disagreement in exchange for staying always-on. Knowing the trade-off lets you state, per feature, which side you want — and that is precisely the input your engineers need.

Concrete next actions you can take this week

  • Find out what you run. Ask your team which database backs your product and whether it is relational (e.g., Postgres, MySQL) or NoSQL (e.g., MongoDB, DynamoDB). One sentence in your notes; it frames every later conversation.
  • Pin down a consistency requirement. For one feature you own, write down whether it needs strong consistency (must always be correct, like money) or can tolerate eventual consistency (a brief lag is fine, like a feed). Share it with engineering.
  • Reframe your next "it's slow" report. The next time a list or search is slow, ask whether an index on the filtered/sorted column would help, rather than just filing "make it faster."
  • Probe one schema change. For an upcoming feature that adds data, ask "does this need a schema migration, and how big is it?" — and notice how the answer changes the estimate.
  • Check the ACID dependency. For any feature touching money, inventory, or bookings, confirm with your team that the operations run inside a transaction with the guarantees you assume.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • How computers and the internet work — the client–server request path that ends at a database query; the natural layer beneath this one.
  • APIs and system design — how application code actually reaches the database, and where caching sits in front of it.
  • Data privacy and security — encryption at rest, access control, and what "the database was breached" really means.
  • Analytics and data warehouses — the OLAP side: how product data flows from the live database into the systems your dashboards read.
  • Cloud infrastructure and cost — managed databases, how replication and storage drive the bill, and the scaling levers behind it.

References

  1. mongodb.com ·cited· (T1)

    ACID is no longer relational-only: 'While early NoSQL databases lacked ACID compliance, modern systems like MongoDB provide full support for ACID transactions at scale.'

  2. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A database is an organized collection of data, and the DBMS is the software that manages it: 'a database is an organized collection of data... based on the use of a database management system (DBMS), the software that interacts with end users, applications, and the database itself to capture and analyze the data.'

  3. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    The relational model, introduced by Edgar Codd in 1969, lets you query data declaratively without knowing how it is stored: it is 'an approach to managing data using a structure and language consistent with first-order predicate logic.'

  4. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A relational database organizes data into tables linked by keys: 'A relational model organizes data into one or more tables (or "relations") of columns and rows, with a unique key identifying each row.'

  5. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A table holds related records in rows and columns: 'a table is a set of data elements (values) using a model of vertical columns and horizontal rows.'

  6. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    SQL is the standard language for querying relational data; it 'introduced the concept of accessing many records with one single command,' a leap over earlier record-at-a-time APIs.

  7. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    The database, not you, decides how to run a query efficiently: 'The purpose of query optimization, which is an automated process, is to find the way to process a given query in minimum time.'

  8. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A schema is the blueprint of the data: 'the schema defines the tables, fields, relationships, views, indexes, packages, procedures, functions, queues, triggers, types, sequences, materialized views, synonyms' and more.

  9. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A primary key is the column(s) that uniquely identifies a row: 'a designated set of attributes (column(s)) that can reliably identify and distinguish between each individual record in a table.'

  10. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A foreign key wires one table to another: it is 'a set of attributes that references a candidate key' — usually the primary key of a related table.

  11. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Normalization splits data to avoid redundancy and update bugs: a key objective is to 'free the collection of relations from undesirable insertion, update and deletion dependencies.'

  12. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    An index is the trade that makes reads fast and writes a little slower: it 'improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a database table at the cost of additional writes and storage space.'

  13. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit of work: it must be 'atomic (it must either be complete in its entirety or have no effect whatsoever).'

  14. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is the reliability contract for transactions: each one 'is treated as a single "unit", which either succeeds completely or fails completely.'

  15. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    NoSQL drops fixed tables for flexible formats and 'scales easily to manage large, often unstructured datasets,' using key-value pairs, documents, and graphs instead of rows and columns.

  16. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Document stores keep each record as a self-contained unit: 'documents as self-contained units that encapsulate and encode data in a standardized format' such as JSON, so different records can have different shapes.

  17. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A key-value store is the simplest NoSQL shape: it treats 'the value as opaque to the database itself, and typically support[s] only simple operations such as storing, retrieving, updating, and deleting a value by its key.'

  18. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Replication keeps copies of the data on multiple servers for availability: 'In single-leader (also called primary/replica) replication, one database instance is designated as the leader (primary), which handles all write operations.'

  19. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Sharding splits the rows themselves across servers to scale out: 'Each shard may be held on a separate database server instance in order to spread [load] across multiple servers.'

  20. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    The CAP theorem names the unavoidable trade-off in distributed databases: 'if there is a network partition, one has to choose between consistency or availability.'

  21. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Eventual consistency trades instant agreement for availability: replicas may briefly disagree, but 'a system that has achieved eventual consistency is said to have converged, or achieved replica convergence.'

  22. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Workload shape decides the database: OLTP handles many small live reads/writes, while 'OLAP is generally optimized for read-only operations' for analytics — the same data often lives in both.

  23. medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    Engineering deep-dive into how Prefixy built a scalable prefix search service to power autocomplete functionality, covering architecture and design decisions.

  24. hellopm.co ·cited· (T3)

    Definitive SQL guide for product managers explaining relational databases, data retrieval, filtering, aggregates, joins, and how SQL enables PMs to run analyses independently and make faster data-driven decisions.

  25. towardsdatascience.com ·cited· (T3)

    Your home for data science and AI. The world’s leading publication for data science, data analytics, data engineering, machine learning, and artificial intelligence professionals.

  26. geeksforgeeks.org ·cited· (T3)

    System design walkthrough for a BookMyShow-style ticket booking platform covering functional and non-functional requirements, seat reservation locking, aggregator-theatre integration strategies, and high-concurrency handling.

  27. technically.dev ·cited· (T3)

    Beginner-friendly guide to SQL covering database schemas, query structure, and core keywords SELECT, FROM, WHERE, and ORDER BY, using a DTC e-commerce example to illustrate concepts.
